questions.question: A 10-cell battery module is tested under two electrical configurations. In the series configuration, thermal runaway (TR) triggered in one cell does not propagate. In the parallel configuration, TR propagates fiercely, with simulation indicating approximately 10°C higher peak temperature attributable to electrical energy transfer from healthy cells into the TR cell. In the parallel module, cells at lower SOC at the time of TR exhibit the longest propagation delay. An engineer is evaluating configuration-dependent TR propagation risk.
options: A. The higher temperature in the parallel module is primarily caused by greater thermal conductivity between cells in the parallel configuration rather than electrical energy redistribution.

B. In the parallel configuration, healthy cells actively contribute energy to the TR event, which is a counterintuitive mechanism that accelerates propagation beyond what thermal conduction alone would produce.
C. The propagation delay observed in lower-SOC cells within the parallel module is consistent with lower available electrochemical energy reducing the driving force for TR progression in those cells.
D. The series configuration's resistance to propagation implies that eliminating electrical interconnection between cells is a sufficient condition to prevent TR propagation in any module geometry.

E. The approximately 10°C temperature elevation from electrical energy transfer in the parallel module represents the total additional thermal load, so thermal management strategies need only account for this increment above the series baseline.

F. Parallel electrical configuration introduces an additional TR propagation pathway through electrical energy redistribution that is absent in series configuration, making configuration a critical design variable for propagation risk.
G. Because TR propagation did not occur in the series module, the series configuration can be considered thermally safe regardless of cell spacing or interstitial material properties.

questions.question: A 100% SOC NCM 811 cylindrical cell with an intact casing undergoes thermal runaway. At the safety valve burst temperature (405.5 K), internal pressure from redox-generated gas is approximately three times the electrolyte vapor pressure. As temperature rises further toward the separator collapsing temperature, the ratio of redox-gas pressure to electrolyte vapor pressure increases to over six. The temperature-to-gas-generation-rate relationship is nonlinear across this range but becomes approximately linear near the separator collapsing temperature. Which of the following conclusions are supported?
options: A. Redox-generated gas pressure is the dominant contributor to safety valve bursting, not electrolyte vapor pressure, at the venting temperature.

B. Because the temperature–gas generation rate relationship is nonlinear before the separator collapsing temperature, a single-step reaction model is sufficient to describe internal pressure buildup in this regime.

C. Setting the safety valve burst threshold substantially above the manufacturer's specified range increases the risk of rupture and explosion because gas generated during thermal runaway cannot be vented in time.

D. The approximately linear relationship between temperature rate and gas generation rate near the separator collapsing temperature implies that a single dominant reaction controls pressure buildup at that stage.
E. At the venting temperature, electrolyte vapor pressure contributes meaningfully to total internal pressure but is not the primary driver of valve bursting.

F. The nonlinear temperature–gas generation relationship before the separator collapsing temperature indicates that total pressure at any intermediate temperature can be accurately predicted from a linear extrapolation of the venting-temperature values.

questions.question: A 1×5 NCM622 cell cluster is operated at varying states of charge. Experimental records show that reducing SOC from 100% to 50% decreases total gas generation from approximately 17.5 mol to 6.2 mol during thermal runaway propagation. At SOC below 25%, no thermal runaway propagation occurs within the cluster. A separate 1×4 cluster test with composite phase-change insulating material between cells achieved complete inhibition of thermal diffusion between cells. An engineer is asked to evaluate design and operational strategies for a module that must operate between 30% and 80% SOC. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Limiting operating SOC to below 25% would reliably prevent thermal runaway propagation in this cluster configuration, but this constraint falls outside the required 30–80% operating range and therefore cannot be applied here.

B. Within the 30–80% SOC operating range, reducing SOC toward the lower bound reduces gas generation during thermal runaway and therefore reduces propagation hazard, even though propagation cannot be fully eliminated by SOC control alone at these levels.

C. The complete inhibition of thermal diffusion achieved with composite phase-change insulating material in a 1×4 cluster is directly transferable to the 1×5 cluster at 80% SOC, eliminating the need for SOC-based operational limits.
D. Because gas generation decreases substantially between 100% and 50% SOC, the relationship between SOC and gas generation is confirmed to be linear across the full SOC range from 0% to 100%.

E. Composite phase-change insulating material represents a more reliable propagation barrier than SOC reduction alone for operation within the 30–80% SOC range, given that SOC reduction within this range cannot achieve the zero-propagation threshold.

F. The zero-propagation threshold at below 25% SOC applies specifically to the tested cluster geometry and chemistry; applying it as a universal design rule for all NCM module configurations is not supported by the evidence.

questions.question: A 2.5 Ah NMC pouch cell undergoes thermal runaway triggered by overheating. At 30% SOC, negligible gas is released and no CO2 is detected. At 100% SOC, gas volume exceeds 6 L and peak reaction temperature surpasses 529 K. An engineer argues that reducing SOC to 30% is sufficient to eliminate explosion hazard. Which statements are admissible given this evidence?
options: A. At 30% SOC, the near-zero gas release and absence of CO2 indicate an incomplete reaction, which mechanistically limits the formation of flammable gas concentrations above the lower explosion limit.
B. At 100% SOC, the gas mixture lower explosion limit of 7 vol% combined with measured CO concentrations above that threshold confirms that ignitable conditions are achievable.
C. Reducing SOC to 30% eliminates thermal runaway risk entirely, since gas emission is negligible and no combustion-relevant species are detectable.
D. The higher peak reaction temperature at 100% SOC is solely caused by increased gas generation, and reducing gas volume would proportionally reduce peak temperature.
E. Because CO2 is absent at 30% SOC, the reaction completeness is lower, but this does not guarantee that all flammable species concentrations fall below their individual explosion limits.
F. The inverse relationship between SOC and reaction completeness means that a cell at 50% SOC will produce exactly half the gas of a cell at 100% SOC.
G. The non-linear increase in gas volume between 50% SOC (1.9 L) and 100% SOC (6.5 L) suggests a threshold-dependent reaction pathway becomes active above 50% SOC.

questions.question: A 21,700 NMC811 cylindrical cell is subjected to an EV-ARC test. The following sequence is observed: self-heating begins near 65°C; at approximately 105°C the temperature rise rate increases sharply and consistently, marking a transition to accelerated heat release; at 143°C the temperature rise rate reaches 1°C/s; the bottom vent ruptures mechanically when internal gas pressure reaches 2 MPa; the gas vented includes hydrogen (~25%), carbon dioxide (~38%), carbon monoxide (~16%), methane (~18%), and ethane (~3%); the maximum temperature reaches 728°C at a peak rate of 7688°C/min. A safety engineer is asked to evaluate which temperature threshold is most appropriate to use as the intervention trigger for a battery management system designed to prevent full thermal runaway propagation in a module.
options: A. The temperature at which self-heating first becomes detectable (~65°C) is the most conservative and therefore most appropriate intervention trigger, because any earlier intervention is always preferable for safety.

B. The threshold at which the temperature rise rate first increases sharply and consistently (~105°C) represents the last point before the system departs from quasi-equilibrium, making it a more mechanistically justified intervention trigger than the 1°C/s threshold.

C. The 1°C/s threshold temperature (~143°C) is the most appropriate intervention trigger because it is the conventional definition of thermal runaway onset and represents a well-defined, measurable transition.

D. Once the temperature rise rate reaches 1°C/s at ~143°C, the subsequent progression to 728°C at 7688°C/min means that any intervention triggered at or after this point must account for the extremely limited time available before peak temperature is reached.

E. The mechanical vent rupture at 2 MPa internal pressure is a reliable early-warning signal that precedes the 1°C/s thermal runaway trigger temperature, and therefore vent rupture detection should replace temperature-based triggering.

F. The gas composition vented during thermal runaway—dominated by carbon dioxide and containing significant hydrogen and carbon monoxide—indicates that both combustion-supporting and flammable species are present, which is relevant to propagation hazard assessment at the module level.

G. Because the maximum temperature (728°C) is more than five times the self-heating onset temperature (65°C in Celsius), the energy released after the 1°C/s trigger point is negligible compared to total stored energy, and intervention at 143°C will reliably prevent significant temperature rise.

questions.question: A 21700 NMC cell at 100% SOC undergoes thermal runaway in an altitude-simulation chamber. As ambient pressure is reduced from 101 kPa to 30 kPa, the following observations are recorded: the onset temperature for thermal runaway increases, the time interval between safety venting and thermal runaway onset shortens from 48 s to 22 s, and the maximum jet flame height is non-monotonic with pressure, peaking at an intermediate pressure rather than at the lowest tested pressure. Mass loss across all tested pressures shows no consistent directional trend. A safety engineer is evaluating whether reduced ambient pressure at high altitude represents a more or less severe thermal runaway hazard compared to sea level. Which of the following statements are admissible based on this evidence?
options: A. The shortened venting-to-runaway interval at lower pressure means that any thermal management response triggered by venting detection has less time to act before runaway onset, increasing operational risk.
B. Because the onset temperature for thermal runaway increases at lower pressure, high-altitude operation is categorically safer than sea-level operation for this cell chemistry.

C. The non-monotonic behavior of jet flame height with pressure indicates that the relationship between ambient pressure and combustion behavior cannot be described by a simple monotonic model over the tested range.
D. The absence of a consistent trend in mass loss across pressures implies that the total energy content released during thermal runaway is strongly pressure-dependent.

E. The rate of temperature rise during thermal runaway is influenced by both internal battery pressure dynamics and external ambient pressure, so neither factor alone fully characterizes the severity of runaway at altitude.
F. Flame lift-off was not observed at any tested pressure, which confirms that jet fire hazard is negligible at reduced ambient pressure.

questions.question: A 21700 NMC cell at 100% SOC undergoes thermal runaway testing across environmental pressures from 101 kPa to 30 kPa. Engineers observe that both the thermal runaway onset temperature and the maximum temperature increase as pressure decreases, while the time interval between safety venting and thermal runaway shortens from 48 s to 22 s. Which conclusions about the pressure-dependent hazard profile are admissible?
options: A. The shortened venting-to-runaway interval at low pressure indicates that despite a higher onset threshold, the effective warning time for intervention is reduced, increasing operational hazard.
B. Because onset temperature rises with decreasing pressure, cells operating at altitude face lower thermal runaway risk than at sea level.
C. The non-monotonic behavior of jet flame height, peaking at an intermediate pressure rather than the lowest tested, suggests that flame hazard cannot be ranked solely by ambient pressure.
D. The near-constant mass loss across all tested pressures implies that the total energy released during thermal runaway is pressure-independent and can be used as a fixed design parameter.
E. Flame lift-off observed at lower pressures for this cell format indicates a transition to a qualitatively different combustion regime below 50 kPa.

F. The simultaneous increase in both onset temperature and maximum temperature with decreasing pressure implies that the internal exothermic reaction kinetics are directly suppressed by reduced oxygen availability.

questions.question: A 300 Ah LFP cell at 100% SOC undergoes thermal runaway triggered by external heating. Surface temperature reaches 593°C. A technician concludes that because flaming combustion was present, it substantially elevated the peak surface temperature compared to a non-ignited test, and that the high surface temperature directly indicates severe internal exothermic reaction comparable to NMC chemistry. Which assessments are admissible given the described conditions?
options: A. The presence of flaming combustion accelerated the onset of thermal runaway but had limited influence on the peak surface temperature value.
B. The 593°C peak surface temperature confirms that the internal temperature also reached approximately 593°C, validating the severity of the internal reaction.

C. The LFP cell's failure to self-ignite under surface temperatures exceeding 600°C in preliminary tests suggests LFP chemistry has a higher resistance to self-sustaining combustion than implied by surface temperature alone.
D. Because mass loss after thermal runaway is independent of ignition state, the presence or absence of flaming combustion can be used to predict total mass ejected from the cell.
E. The peak surface temperature of 593°C at 100% SOC is consistent with an SOC-dependent trend, where lower SOC cells would exhibit lower peak surface temperatures under equivalent heating.
F. The peak surface temperature is primarily controlled by the combustion heat release rate, so suppressing ignition would reduce peak surface temperature proportionally.

questions.question: A 38 Ah NCM battery is characterized using both accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) of extracted electrode materials. ARC measurements use surface-mounted temperature sensors. DSC analysis of the cathode material shows a structural phase transition at 300°C and a second phase transition at 400°C. Separate electrode-only DSC experiments show the cathode alone undergoes thermal runaway at 189°C and the anode alone at approximately 240°C. The total heat generation calculated from DSC material analysis is 371,087 J, while the total heat generation calculated from ARC surface temperature measurements is 314,296 J. The battery does not undergo thermal runaway at 0% or 25% SOC but does at SOC values above 35%. The self-heat rate threshold defining the thermal runaway triggering temperature is 10°C/min. An engineer is evaluating the heat source hierarchy and measurement limitations for this system. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. The fact that either electrode alone can undergo thermal runaway without the other electrode present indicates that internal short circuit is not required as the primary initiating heat source for thermal runaway in this system.

B. The ARC-derived total heat generation being lower than the DSC-derived value is best explained by the ARC surface temperature underestimating the internal temperature, causing the ARC calculation to undercount heat released near the cell core.

C. The structural phase transition of the cathode material at 300°C is the event that defines the thermal runaway triggering temperature in ARC testing, since it represents the onset of irreversible cathode decomposition.

D. Because the SEI decomposition and regeneration occur simultaneously between 100°C and 270°C, a battery whose surface temperature is measured at 200°C during ARC testing has definitively broken the SEI decomposition–regeneration balance.

E. The SOC threshold above which thermal runaway occurs (35%) implies that the electrochemical energy stored above this threshold is necessary to sustain the exothermic reaction cascade, and batteries below this threshold cannot generate sufficient heat from electrode reactions alone.

F. The cathode phase transition from rhombohedral to spinel at 300°C and from LiM2O4-type spinel to Co3O4-type spinel at 400°C occur above the measured thermal runaway triggering temperature of 188°C, indicating these transitions are consequences of thermal runaway progression rather than its initiating cause.
G. The difference between DSC-derived and ARC-derived heat totals cannot be used to determine the exact internal temperature profile of the battery during thermal runaway.

questions.question: A 3×3 module of cylindrical lithium-ion cells undergoes impact-induced thermal runaway in the central cell. Thermography confirms the impacted cell's internal contents exceed 800°C and flame temperatures surpass 500°C. Thermocouples in interstitial spaces record a brief plateau near 34°C followed by a surge to 250°C, then decay. Post-event thermography shows all surrounding cells remain at room temperature. Which conclusions about heat transfer and propagation behavior are supported by these observations?
options: A. The 250°C interstitial temperature surge confirms that neighbouring cells experienced internal exothermic reactions during the event.
B. The plateau period in the interstitial temperature profile is consistent with a transient thermal equilibration before the main gas and flame release reaches the surrounding space.

C. The fact that neighbouring cells remained at room temperature despite the central cell exceeding 800°C indicates that the geometry of cylindrical cell packing limits the conductive heat pathway between cells.
D. Because flame temperature exceeded 500°C, thermal runaway propagation to adjacent cells is thermodynamically guaranteed if contact time is sufficient.
E. The interstitial temperature reaching 250°C is sufficient evidence to conclude that neighbouring cells were brought to a state of imminent thermal runaway.
F. The absence of thermal runaway in neighbouring cells despite extreme local temperatures is consistent with restricted contact area reducing the effective heat flux into adjacent cells.
G. The thermography measurement of internal battery content temperature above 800°C directly quantifies the peak cathode decomposition temperature during the event.

questions.question: A 4 Ah lithium iron phosphate cell undergoes thermal runaway inside an 80 L enclosed chamber. Measurements confirm that the released gas explosion propagates at supersonic speed and produces an average maximum overpressure of 383 kPa in the closed configuration. An explosion vent is then installed on the chamber. Tests with varying vent sizes show that as the dimensionless venting coefficient Kv decreases from 74.26 to 6.19, both peak overpressure and deflagration index decrease substantially. However, a venting efficiency index that accounts for both overpressure reduction and vent area cost reaches a maximum at an intermediate Kv value of 8.25 before declining. An engineer evaluates whether increasing vent area indefinitely is the optimal design strategy. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Increasing vent area beyond the optimal Kv value of 8.25 continues to reduce overpressure but does so with diminishing returns in venting efficiency, making arbitrarily large vents suboptimal by the efficiency criterion.

B. The supersonic propagation speed measured in the closed chamber confirms that the explosion regime is detonation, which means the explosion vent must be designed to contain rather than relieve the pressure wave to be effective.

C. At small vent areas, the risk of secondary explosion may increase relative to intermediate vent sizes, because delayed vent opening allows greater accumulation of combustible gases that can mix with external air after venting.

D. Because the explosion vent reduced average maximum overpressure by over 93%, it can be concluded that the explosion vent eliminates the structural risk to the chamber under all TR gas release scenarios.

E. The optimal venting coefficient Kv = 8.25 represents a design point where the tradeoff between overpressure reduction and vent area cost is most favorable, but this optimum is specific to the tested configuration and may not transfer directly to chambers of different volume or cell capacity.

F. Reducing Kv from 74.26 to 6.19 produces a monotonic decrease in both peak overpressure and deflagration index, which implies that the minimum achievable overpressure is obtained at the smallest possible Kv value regardless of other design constraints.

questions.question: A 50 Ah prismatic LFP cell at 25°C is being overcharged under constant-current conditions. Surface infrared monitoring shows that below 100% SOC, the thermal pattern is a stable lateral stratification aligned with the tab region, with a maximum surface temperature difference of 18°C. As overcharging continues to 105% SOC, the thermal pattern transitions to a concentric distribution centered on the middle of the cell face, and this transition precedes any visible mechanical deformation by approximately 38 seconds. Beyond 120% SOC, the surface temperature rise rate increases sharply relative to the slow rate observed between 90% and 120% SOC. An engineer proposes using the thermal pattern transition at 105% SOC as the primary intervention trigger. Which of the following conclusions about this monitoring strategy and the underlying thermal behavior are supported by the described evidence?
options: A. The shift from lateral to concentric thermal distribution at 105% SOC provides a warning signal that precedes the onset of visible mechanical deformation, making it a structurally earlier indicator than naked-eye inspection of cell bulging.
B. Because the concentric hotspot pattern appears before visible deformation, it can be concluded that internal gas generation has not yet begun at the moment the thermal pattern changes.

C. The lateral thermal stratification below 100% SOC is attributable to current concentration effects near the tab geometry, and its disappearance at 105% SOC is consistent with a redistribution of internal heat sources away from the tab region.

D. The sharp increase in temperature rise rate above 120% SOC confirms that the thermal behavior between 90% and 120% SOC is governed by the same mechanism as that above 120% SOC, differing only in rate.

E. Using the thermal pattern transition as an intervention trigger is more conservative than waiting for the sharp temperature rise rate increase, since the pattern transition occurs at a lower SOC than the rate acceleration threshold.

F. The 38-second lead time over visible deformation is sufficient to guarantee that a discharge intervention initiated at the pattern transition will prevent irreversible capacity loss in all LFP cell formats.

G. The tab-aligned lateral stratification during normal charging and its replacement by a central hotspot at 105% SOC represent two distinct thermal regimes whose boundary is defined by the overcharge threshold, not by the absolute surface temperature value.

questions.question: A 53 Ah NCM pouch cell at 100% SOC is subjected separately to three thermal runaway triggers: nail penetration, lateral external heating, and overcharge at 1C. Measurements show that the internal temperature during nail penetration reaches approximately 1005 °C while the surface temperature peaks at 774 °C. Under lateral heating, the surface trigger temperature is 233 °C and thermal runaway spreads to the rear surface in approximately 20 s. Under overcharge, thermal runaway is triggered at a surface temperature of 117 °C after the cell has been charged to approximately 128% SOC. An engineer must assess which diagnostic and mechanistic conclusions are supported by these observations. Which of the following are admissible conclusions?
options: A. For nail penetration, surface temperature measurement alone is insufficient to characterize the peak thermal state of the cell interior during thermal runaway, because the internal temperature substantially exceeds the surface temperature.
B. The lower surface trigger temperature under overcharge compared to lateral heating indicates that overcharge generates less total heat than lateral heating before thermal runaway onset.
C. The longer injection duration under overcharge compared to nail penetration is consistent with a greater cumulative energy input to the cell before thermal runaway is triggered.
D. The 20-second propagation time across the cell thickness under lateral heating implies that the same propagation rate would be observed in a multi-cell module under identical heating conditions.

E. Under nail penetration, the fact that internal temperature rise precedes surface temperature rise indicates that heat originates internally and diffuses outward, rather than being imposed from the cell surface.
F. The surface trigger temperature under overcharge being lower than under lateral heating is sufficient to conclude that overcharge is the least hazardous of the three abuse modes tested.

questions.question: A 9-cell battery module is assembled using cells with a high-safety electrolyte (HSE) that is non-flammable and raises the thermal runaway trigger temperature by ~24°C relative to base electrolyte (BE). Under a thermal abuse scenario initiating runaway in cell 1, only three cells trigger runaway with HSE versus full propagation through all nine cells with BE. The maximum module temperature with HSE reaches ~203°C in cell 4. Which conclusions are admissible regarding HSE module-level behavior?
options: A. HSE eliminates thermal runaway propagation entirely in the module because its non-flammability prevents heat transfer between cells.
B. HSE arrests propagation after three cells, indicating that the non-flammable electrolyte reduces but does not eliminate inter-cell thermal coupling under these conditions.
C. The ~24°C increase in trigger temperature is the sole mechanism responsible for propagation arrest, since flammability plays no independent role.

D. The maximum temperature of ~203°C in cell 4 with HSE suggests that cells beyond cell 3 received insufficient thermal input to reach their elevated trigger threshold, making propagation arrest a threshold-dependent outcome.
E. Because HSE cells still undergo thermal runaway in three cells, the electrolyte change provides no meaningful safety benefit at the module level.
F. The propagation behavior difference between BE and HSE cannot be attributed to electrolyte properties alone without ruling out differences in cell spacing or thermal management.

questions.question: A CFD model is used to predict explosion pressure in a 1-m channel filled with Li-ion vent gas. Experiments at equivalence ratio 1.08 yield a maximum pressure of 1.36 kPa (gauge). The CFD simulation predicts 1.45 kPa (gauge) at equivalence ratio 1.0. Separately, the simulation fails to reproduce pressure and flame front velocity oscillations observed as the flame approaches the channel opening. An engineer must judge which aspects of the simulation are validated and which are not.
options: A. The CFD model can be considered validated for peak explosion pressure prediction within the tested channel geometry, since predicted and measured peak pressures agree within approximately 7%.

B. Because the simulation underpredicts peak pressure relative to experiment at a slightly different equivalence ratio, the model systematically underestimates explosion hazard across all equivalence ratios.
C. The failure to reproduce oscillatory behavior near the channel opening means the simulation cannot be relied upon to predict dynamic pressure loading on structures located near the vent opening.
D. Agreement in peak pressure magnitude alone is sufficient to validate the model for predicting flame propagation dynamics throughout the entire channel.

E. The discrepancy in equivalence ratio between peak pressure predictions (1.0 vs. 1.08) indicates the model's stoichiometric sensitivity differs from the experiment, which is a limitation relevant to mixture composition uncertainty.
F. Since peak pressures differ by less than 10%, the oscillation prediction failure is inconsequential for any engineering application of this model.

questions.question: A CNN-LSTM detection system monitors temperature contour images from a battery pack to identify abnormal heat generation. The system uses sequences of frames at a fixed time step; testing showed that increasing the time step from 8 s to 16 s per sequence reduced validation accuracy from approximately 97% to 89%, attributed to reduced contrast between risk and safe states and increased temperature change per step. The system achieves a recall of 100% and an inference time under 0.1 s per batch. A separate finding shows that when two cells undergo thermal runaway simultaneously rather than one, detection performance improves because the temperature contrast in the monitored domain becomes more prominent. An engineer proposes deploying this system as the primary safety response trigger for a pack, arguing that 100% recall and sub-0.1 s inference time together guarantee that no thermal runaway event will go undetected in time for intervention. Which of the following judgments about this proposal are correct?
options: A. The 100% recall result is specific to the experimental conditions under which the model was trained and evaluated; applying it as a guarantee across all deployment configurations requires assuming that operational temperature contrast patterns remain within the training distribution.

B. The improvement in detection performance when two cells are in thermal runaway rather than one is counterintuitive from a safety perspective, because the condition that improves detection is itself a more severe failure state.
C. The sub-0.1 s inference time is sufficient to guarantee timely intervention because it is negligible compared to the timescales of thermal runaway propagation in typical battery modules.

D. The degradation in accuracy when the time step is doubled suggests that the model's performance is sensitive to the temporal resolution of the input, which means detection reliability could degrade if the monitoring system's sampling rate changes in deployment.

E. Because recall is 100%, the F1-score of 85.02 must imply a precision below 100%, meaning the system produces false positives; deploying it as the sole trigger without false-positive filtering could cause unnecessary safety activations.

F. The detection system's performance characteristics are sufficient to conclude that it can replace electrochemical state monitoring because temperature contour imaging captures all relevant precursors to thermal runaway.

questions.question: A CTP battery module undergoes thermal runaway in a closed nitrogen-filled enclosure instrumented with pressure and voltage sensors. Engineers observe that abrupt chamber pressure changes occur slightly before the recorded thermal runaway trigger times for each cell in the module. The vented gas composition is dominated by H2, CO2, and CO, with a calculated explosion limit substantially wider than methane and a lower explosive limit near 6%. Which conclusions about monitoring strategy and gas hazard are admissible given these conditions?
options: A. Pressure monitoring in the enclosed space provides earlier warning of thermal runaway onset than voltage monitoring alone.
B. The wide explosion limit range and low explosive limit of the vented gas indicate that the gas mixture poses a higher explosion risk upon air ingress than a pure methane leak of equivalent volume.
C. Because the enclosure is filled with nitrogen, the vented gas cannot form a flammable mixture and the explosion limit calculation is irrelevant to operational safety.
D. The dominance of H2 in the vented gas composition is consistent with conditions of advanced thermal runaway or deflagration rather than initial venting stages where CO2 typically dominates.
E. The higher TRP temperature and lower mass loss observed in the closed nitrogen environment compared to open air indicate that nitrogen confinement reduces the severity of thermal runaway.

F. Since pressure change precedes voltage-based TR detection by only a small margin, voltage monitoring remains sufficient as the primary safety signal and pressure monitoring adds no operationally meaningful lead time.

questions.question: A CuO-decorated InSe monolayer sensor system is proposed for detecting lithium battery thermal runaway gases. At 298 K, CO desorption time is ~6.6×10¹² s, while CO₂ desorption time is extremely short. CuO decoration increases CO adsorption by 130% but significantly reduces CO₂ adsorption versus pristine InSe. An engineer must assign functional roles to this material. Which assignments are admissible?
options: A. CuO-InSe is suitable as a CO adsorbent because its extremely long CO desorption time at 298 K indicates strong, sustained capture.
B. CuO-InSe is suitable as a CO₂ sensor for early thermal runaway detection because its rapid CO₂ response enables fast signal generation despite weak adsorption.
C. CuO-InSe should be used as a CO₂ adsorbent in scrubbing applications because CuO decoration enhances overall gas capture capability.
D. The band gap reduction from 1.938 eV to 0.831 eV upon CuO decoration directly explains the enhanced electronic sensitivity that underpins improved CO detection.
E. Because desorption times decrease with temperature, CO and CO₂ cannot be distinguished by CuO-InSe under any thermal management strategy.
F. The functional role of CuO-InSe differs fundamentally between CO and CO₂: strong adsorbent for the former, rapid-response sensor for the latter.

questions.question: A LiCoO2/graphite pouch cell undergoes mechanical indentation at 5% SOC, producing an internal short circuit with only 11°C temperature rise and no thermal runaway. The same cell at 95% SOC under identical indentation triggers thermal runaway, reaching ~650°C with separator collapse and current collector melting. The critical SOC threshold for TR under mechanical abuse for this chemistry is approximately 0.6. A designer proposes that operating below 60% SOC eliminates mechanical-abuse-induced TR risk for all LIB chemistries. Which options correctly evaluate this proposal?
options: A. The proposal is inadmissible because the 0.6 SOC threshold is chemistry- and structure-specific and cannot be generalized to all LIB chemistries.
B. The proposal is partially supported because the 5% SOC result demonstrates that very low SOC prevents TR under mechanical abuse, but the exact safe threshold differs by system.
C. The 11°C temperature rise at 5% SOC confirms that internal short circuit alone cannot trigger TR regardless of chemistry, making the proposal universally valid.

D. Operating below 60% SOC reduces but does not eliminate TR risk for the studied chemistry, because the threshold defines the boundary between ISC-dominated and TR-dominated responses for that specific system.
E. The proposal is inadmissible because at low SOC the dominant failure mode shifts from thermal runaway to prolonged internal discharge, which may cause different but non-negligible safety hazards.
F. Since thermal runaway at 95% SOC reaches 650°C while 5% SOC shows only 11°C rise, a linear interpolation of temperature rise with SOC is the most appropriate model for TR risk assessment.
